one_line_takeaway: A rigorous re-evaluation showing that leading prompt-injection defenses (StruQ, SecAlign, Instruction Hierarchy, PromptGuard, Attention Tracker) are substantially less effective and utility-preserving than their own papers claim once diverse prompts, absolute-utility/FPR/FNR metrics, and adaptive attacks are used.
key_finding: Under principled evaluation the recent defenses hold up far worse than claimed. StruQ/SecAlign lose real utility (win rate vs Llama-3-8B-Instruct only 21.60% / 36.42%; absolute utility drops of 0.11/0.17 on OpenPromptInjection). GCG already reaches high ASV against them (StruQ 0.80 on OpenPromptInjection, 0.88 on MMLU-PI; SecAlign 0.72 on MMLU-PI), and adaptive GCG pushes StruQ to ASV 1.00 on both benchmarks. Instruction Hierarchy as deployed on GPT-4o-mini is broken by even the existing Combined Attack (ASV 0.68 / 0.75). For detectors, high AUC hides failure: PromptGuard has AUC 0.92 but FPR 0.89 on OpenPromptInjection (flags almost everything), and Attention Tracker has AUC 1.00 yet FNR 0.69 on MMLU-PI; the adaptive attack raises Attention Tracker's FNR to 0.96 (OpenPromptInjection) and 1.00 (MMLU-PI), with every evading sample also successfully executing the injected task.
core_contribution: This is a critical-evaluation (meta) paper, not a new defense or attack. Its own contribution is a principled methodology for evaluating prompt-injection defenses along two dimensions - effectiveness (against both existing AND adaptive attacks, over diverse target/injected prompts) and general-purpose utility (absolute task utility, not just relative win rate) - plus (1) a newly constructed MMLU-PI benchmark, (2) new adaptive attacks tailored to specific defenses (an adaptive GCG for StruQ/SecAlign, and a general adaptive-attack framework for detectors), and (3) a re-evaluation showing that recent defenses are far less successful than their original papers claim. Crucially, the defenses being scrutinized - StruQ, SecAlign, Instruction Hierarchy, PromptGuard, Attention Tracker - are all PRIOR work being tested, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: Standard prompt injection: an LLM prompt is instruction||data; when the data comes from an untrusted source (web, tool output, reviews), an attacker embeds an injected prompt pe into the data using a separator z, so the contaminated input pt||z||pe drives the LLM to perform an attacker-chosen injected task instead of the intended target task. The paper considers both heuristic attacks (Naive, Context-Ignoring, Fake-Completion, Escape-Character, Combined) and optimization-based attacks (GCG optimizing the separator/injected prompt), and stresses that a rigorous evaluation must also include ADAPTIVE attacks crafted against the specific defense.
limitations: Evaluation is confined to two benchmarks and a small model scale (Llama-3-8B family, Qwen2-1.5B for Attention Tracker; GPT-4o-mini the only large/proprietary system). For compute reasons GCG runs on only 50 tuples (OpenPromptInjection) / 25 tuples (MMLU-PI), a small sample for the optimization-based ASVs. Instruction Hierarchy is assessed only as a black box via GPT-4o-mini since the training details are undisclosed, so results reflect the deployed product rather than the method in isolation. The paper proposes no new defense - it is diagnostic - and the adaptive attacks, while effective, are demonstrated against a limited defense set. Single-GPU setup bounds attack budget and generality of the negative results.

datasets_used: OpenPromptInjection (7 NLP tasks: duplicate-sentence detection, grammar correction, hate-speech detection, NLI, sentiment analysis, spam detection, summarization; 700 prompt-response pairs, T=4,900 tuples, X=700 clean samples, Xc=4,900 contaminated pairs)
MMLU-PI (newly constructed from MMLU test split; 200 prompt-response pairs, T=1,000 tuples, X=200 clean samples, Xc=1,000 contaminated pairs)
AlpacaFarm / AlpacaEval (used for relative win-rate utility, GPT-4-Turbo judge)
quantitative_results: Win rate vs Llama-3-8B-Instruct: StruQ 21.60%, SecAlign 36.42% (indicates utility loss, not preservation)
Absolute utility (OpenPromptInjection / MMLU-PI): Llama-3-8B-Instruct 0.65 / 0.45; StruQ 0.54 / 0.35; SecAlign 0.48 / 0.34 (drops of 0.11/0.10 for StruQ, 0.17/0.11 for SecAlign)
ASV StruQ OpenPromptInjection: Combined existing 0.03, adaptive 0.09; GCG existing 0.80, adaptive 1.00
ASV StruQ MMLU-PI: Combined 0.10 / adaptive 0.16; GCG existing 0.88, adaptive 1.00
ASV SecAlign OpenPromptInjection: Combined 0.06 / adaptive 0.04; GCG existing 0.24, adaptive 0.46
ASV SecAlign MMLU-PI: Combined 0.14 / adaptive 0.13; GCG existing 0.72, adaptive 0.88
Instruction Hierarchy (GPT-4o-mini): absolute utility 0.71 (OpenPromptInjection) / 0.73 (MMLU-PI); Combined Attack ASV 0.68 / 0.75
PromptGuard: AUC 0.92 but FPR 0.89, FNR 0.00 (OpenPromptInjection); AUC 0.75, FPR 0.84, FNR 0.00 (MMLU-PI)
Attention Tracker: AUC 1.00 on both; FPR 0.00, FNR 0.00 (OpenPromptInjection); FPR 0.00, FNR 0.69 (MMLU-PI)
Adaptive attack FNR vs Attention Tracker (OpenPromptInjection / MMLU-PI): Separator 0.66 / 1.00; Separator+Instruction 0.96 / 1.00; Separator+Instruction+Data 0.96 / 1.00
Appendix (Llama-3-8B base variants): StruQ/SecAlign win rates near 50% (48.04% / 55.03%) vs undefended; adaptive GCG reaches ASV 1.00 (StruQ) and 0.70/0.88 (SecAlign)
baselines_compared: StruQ (supervised fine-tuning, prior work) - prevention-based defense evaluated
SecAlign (DPO fine-tuning, prior work) - prevention-based defense evaluated
Instruction Hierarchy (Wallace et al., deployed on GPT-4o-mini) - prevention-based defense evaluated
PromptGuard (Meta, mDeBERTa-v3 classifier) - detection-based defense evaluated
Attention Tracker (attention-pattern detector on Qwen2-1.5B) - detection-based defense evaluated
Attacks used as probes: Combined Attack (heuristic) and GCG (optimization-based), each in existing and adaptive forms
